notes.text: Hook: Katniss survives in District 12 by hunting illegally and protecting her family under a brutal political system.
Resourceful survivor whose first priority is family, not revolution.
Personal survival and political oppression are inseparable in Panem.
Plot Turn 1: Prim is reaped; Katniss volunteers and enters the Games, crossing from local hardship to national spectacle.
Her vulnerability defines Katniss's core motivation and moral line.
Pinch Point 1: In training and interviews, the Capitol's control and the lethal nature of the arena become undeniable.
Teaches performance strategy: in the Games, image and sponsors can be as important as combat skill.
Peeta's public declaration of love reframes Katniss's strategy around narrative and audience perception.
Midpoint: Inside the arena, Katniss chooses alliance and compassion (Rue, medicine plan) instead of pure self-preservation.
Her partnership and death transform Katniss's arc from survival to moral resistance.
Human solidarity becomes an act of rebellion against dehumanizing spectacle.
Pinch Point 2: Rule changes and arena manipulations intensify pressure; the Capitol repeatedly rewrites conditions to maintain control.
Katniss and Peeta's 'star-crossed lovers' performance becomes both tactical shield and emotional complication.
Represents the system's insistence that every human relationship be turned into control or spectacle.
Plot Turn 2: Final confrontation narrows to Katniss and Peeta versus Cato and engineered threats; the endgame is explicit and unavoidable.
Mutts and final arena sequencing visualize the Capitol's willingness to script violence to the last second.
Resolution: Faced with being forced to kill each other, Katniss and Peeta threaten double suicide with nightlock, forcing a political retreat.
Katniss wins by refusing the moral premise of the Games, not just by out-fighting opponents.
After victory, uncertainty remains: private feelings, public narrative, and Capitol retaliation set up the longer saga arc.
